subject: [PATCH 0/7 v2] mm/memcontrol, page_counter: move stock from mem_cgroup to page_counter

reviews.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
reviews.summary: The author is addressing concerns about the current implementation of per-cpu stocks in memcontrol, specifically the random eviction problem and tight coupling with struct mem_cgroup. The author agrees that these issues can be addressed by pushing the concept of stock down to the page_counter level, which would eliminate the 7-memcg-per-cpu slot limit and make enabling separate stock caches for other page_counters simpler.

reviews.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
reviews.summary: The author addressed a concern about the page_counter_stock being unused and not enabled on any memcg yet, stating that no functional changes are intended.

reviews.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
reviews.summary: The author addressed a concern about the need for synchronization in stock draining, introduced two new functions to drain stock: page_counter_drain_stock_local() and page_counter_drain_stock_cpu(), which take a local lock or not respectively, depending on the context. The author did not explicitly state that they will restructure the code in v2, but the introduction of these new functions suggests that further revision may be necessary.

reviews.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
reviews.summary: Author addressed a concern about the potential for stale stock on large machines by explaining that the new design's overcharge bound is nr_leaf_cgroups x nr_cpus x 64 pages, and arguing that this should not be a problem as long as cgroups are actively charging memory. The author also noted that the system can continue to operate with the page counter working without stock and falling back to walking the hierarchy if there is not enough memory to allocate a percpu page_counter_stock.

reviews.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
reviews.summary: Author addressed a concern about the lack of a fast path for cgroup v1's memsw check after moving the per-cpu stock to page_counter, explained that introducing an independent stock for memsw provides better caching and avoids the worst-case scenario where both counters must walk two hierarchies. The author confirmed this change will be included in the next version.

reviews.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
reviews.summary: The author addressed a concern about wasted memory in parent cgroups due to rare direct charges, and explained that draining the stock when the first child is created can return pages for other memcgs to use. This optimization is specific to cgroup v2, as tasks can be attached to any cgroup in the hierarchy in v1.
reviews.raw_body:
In cgroup v2, it is unlikely for memcg charges to happen directly on
non-leaf cgroups. There are a few exceptions, such as processes that
have yet to be migrated to children, and tasks that are reparented on
memcg destruction, that charge to the parent.

Because it is rare for parent cgroups to receive direct charges, stock
that remains in them are wasted memory.

Drain parent stocks when the first child is created to return those
pages for other memcgs to use.

This optimization is not for cgroup v1, where tasks can be attached to
any cgroup in the hierarchy, meaning stock can be consumed & refilled
for non-leaf cgroups as well.

Signed-off-by: Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com>
---
 mm/memcontrol.c | 15 +++++++++++++++
 1 file changed, 15 insertions(+)

diff --git a/mm/memcontrol.c b/mm/memcontrol.c
index 06ec4d26cb519..fc1e1b10b6ab6 100644
--- a/mm/memcontrol.c
+++ b/mm/memcontrol.c
@@ -3968,6 +3968,21 @@ static int mem_cgroup_css_online(struct cgroup_subsys_state *css)
 	 */
 	xa_store(&mem_cgroup_private_ids, memcg->id.id, memcg, GFP_KERNEL);
 
+	/*
+	 * It is unlikely for non-leaf memcgs to get direct charges on v2.
+	 * Drain the parent's stock if we are the first child.
+	 */
+	if (cgroup_subsys_on_dfl(memory_cgrp_subsys)) {
+		struct mem_cgroup *parent = parent_mem_cgroup(memcg);
+		int cpu;
+
+		if (parent && !mem_cgroup_is_root(parent) &&
+			      !css_has_online_children(&parent->css)) {
+			for_each_online_cpu(cpu)
+				work_on_cpu(cpu, drain_stock_on_cpu, parent);
+		}
+	}
+
 	return 0;
 offline_kmem:
 	memcg_offline_kmem(memcg);
-- 
2.53.0-Meta

reviews.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
reviews.summary: The author acknowledged that the memcg_stock logic has been moved to page_counter_stock, and as a result, all code related to handling memcg_stock can be removed. The obj_stock is untouched and still needed.

reviews.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
reviews.summary: Author acknowledges a mistake in patch development process, proposes rebasing on mm-new and sending as v3, indicating a fix is planned for the next version.
reviews.raw_body:
This iteration was developed and tested on top of mm-stable.
I'm seeing now that Sashiko cannot apply this patch, and I think it expects
it to have been built on top of mm-new.

Reviewers -- would it make sense to rebase this on top of mm-new and
re-send this as a v3, or should I wait for feedback on this cycle
before sending out a new version?

In any case, this could have been avoided if I just developed on top of
mm-new. I'll be mindful to do that in the future.

Thank you everyone,
Joshua

reviews.author: Andrew Morton
reviews.summary: Reviewer Andrew Morton suggested resending the patch for Sashiko review and human review, citing the rapidly growing mm-new branch and potential value in parallelizing reviews.
reviews.raw_body:
Sashiko does attempt various branches, including mm-stable so I'm not
sure what went wrong here.

It's a bit of a crapshoot at this time, as mm-new is still growing like
a weed (19 patches yesterday, 24 so far today).  Slowing way down soon!
I'll be pushing mm-new later this evening and after that it shouldn't change
much for several days because we all take weekends off, don't we?

Convention appears to say "wait a week before resending" but IMO
there's value in parallelizing Sashiko review with human review.  And
it's understandable if some humans aren't very motivated to review
until the AI thing has had a shot at it, because that might result in
alterations.  So I'd say that a resend-for-Sashiko is appropriate.

2026-05-22.patch_summary: This patch series moves the per-cpu stock from mem_cgroup to page_counter, allowing for more efficient and scalable memory management. The current implementation has limitations, including a 7-memcg-per-cpu slot limit and random evictions of stock when multiple memcgroups are charging on a single CPU. By moving the stock to page_counter, these issues are resolved, enabling independent stock management for additional page_counters in each memcg. This change also simplifies the code and makes it easier to follow. However, there are some tradeoffs, including an increased bound on overcharged memory and additional memory footprint due to a new struct page_counter_stock.
